auction(W, P, O) :- winners(W), price(P), order(O).

% Enforce that each winner has exactly one auction (one price and one order).
:- not 1 { auction(W, P, O) : price(P), order(O) } 1 :- winners(W).

% Enforce that each price is used exactly once.
:- not 1 { auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), order(O) } 1 :- price(P).

% Enforce that each order is used exactly once.
:- not 1 { auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), price(P) } 1 :- order(O).

% 1. The butterfly that was auctioned 7th sold for 20 dollars less than the insect won by Daniel.
:- auction(W, P, 7), auction("Daniel", Pd, _), P != Pd - 20.

% 2. The four butterflies were the insect that sold for $260, the butterfly that was auctioned 4th, the butterfly that was auctioned 7th, and the butterfly won by Roland.
% This means: the lot with price 260, the lot with order 4, the lot with order 7, and the lot with winner Roland are all distinct.
% So we ensure that no two of these properties are satisfied by the same lot.
:- auction(W, 260, O), auction(W, P, 4), O = 4.           % $260 lot is also 4th
:- auction(W, 260, O), auction("Roland", P, O).           % $260 lot is Roland's
:- auction(W, 260, O), auction(W, P, 7), O = 7.           % $260 lot is also 7th
:- auction(W, P, 4), auction(W, P, 7).                   % 4th lot is also 7th
:- auction(W, P, 4), auction(W, P, O), W = "Roland".      % 4th lot is Roland's
:- auction(W, P, 7), auction(W, P, O), W = "Roland".      % 7th lot is Roland's

% 3. The butterfly won by Gabriel was the 8th lot.
:- auction("Gabriel", P, O), O != 8.

players("Charles"; "Evan"; "Karl"; "Vincent").
games(8; 9; 10; 11).
positions("center field"; "first base"; "shortstop"; "third base").

% For each player, assign exactly one position and one number of games.

% Each position is assigned to exactly one player.
